steps.title: Connect travel to homeland
steps.content: The question asks for the function of the underlined lines within the poem as a whole. The opening stanza establishes the speaker wandering in "alien lands". The underlined lines shift inward: "But in my soul", "The eastern billows roll—", and "I hear the voices of my native strands." The correct answer must describe what those internal images do for the poem.

steps.title: Rule out off-target choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'It establishes that the speaker's enthusiasm about current travels conflicts with the growing urge to return home.': the opening stanza does not express enthusiasm; it describes alien lands where Nature yields to Man, implying detachment or critique. No enthusiasm is present to conflict with anything.
  • Choice B — 'It illustrates the speaker's uncertainty about maintaining strong links with relatives in distant places.': the underlined lines mention "native strands" (homeland), not family members or relational ties. Relatives are never referenced.

steps.title: Rule out the remaining choices
steps.content: • Choice D — 'It reveals that upon returning after an extended absence, the speaker longs for the way a location once felt.': the speaker has not returned home; the poem explicitly states "I wander among the hills of alien lands", meaning the speaker is still abroad.

steps.title: Choose the pull toward home
steps.content:
• Choice C — 'It conveys the speaker's sense of feeling a pull toward home while traveling in an unfamiliar place.': matches precisely — the rolling "eastern billows" in the soul and the voices of "native strands" express an emotional draw toward the homeland while the speaker is abroad.
The answer is C.
